issues.title: [Windows][Accessibility]: Text field input is read inconsistently while adding, deleting and while using shift / arrow keys.
issues.body:
Text fields are basically unusable, and it is often to write text in some other program, then paste it into Flutter apps.

I could have sworn there was already an issue for this, but I am unable to find it, so sorry if this is a duplicate.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Create an app with the below code.
2. Run `flutter run -d windows`.
3. With a screen reader (such as [NVDA](https://www.nvaccess.org/download)), enter some text, navigate through the text with the arrow keys, try and find an arbitrary point to add more text, ETC.
## Problems

- Moving through the text field with the arrow keys (or home and end) speaks nothing (#95556).
- When typing new characters, the text of the field is spoken in its entirety.
- When deleting text with the backspace or delete keys, the text is spoken in its entirety.
- When selecting text with shift and the arrow keys, nothing is spoken.

I am not observing all this same behavior in the repro on Windows. When typing a new character only the new character is read, and the new text is read if the user does anything to trigger the text field to be re-read (e.g. Narrator+UP or hover cursor)

In https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/main/shell/platform/windows/accessibility_bridge_delegate_windows.cc#L95 it appears we effectively just ignore `DOCUMENT_SELECTION_CHANGED` events. I see a few possible Windows event constants that may be appropriate, here particularly `EVENT_OBJECT_SELECTION` and `EVENT_OBJECT_TEXTSELECTIONCHANGED`.

@yaakovschectman 
Narrator does that when pressing letter keys in Spotify as well. It's just repeating the key you've pressed, not reading anything meaningful.

Narrator is really only used by people setting up their computers for the first time, before switching to their daily driver. If you wish to test things with a screen reader, I'd recommend [NVDA](https://www.nvaccess.org/), which is free and open source, and used in the wild. Furthermore, you should turn off character echo (insert + 2 with NVDA), since that's where you see the real problems.

Honestly, if someone has such poor site they are using a screen reader, they will probably not be using the mouse to trigger anything, so the arrow keys are far more representative of actual blind users. Obviously I can't speak for people with other disabilities and using other assistive technologies.

So far in investigating this, I have that when a text field is selected/focused, while Inspect and the debug Python console in NVDA report that the text field node is `focused`, the `client (role 0xA)` node that contains it is what NVDA treats as the focused component, so certain edit messages are not being fired.

Just tested the code above with the latest Flutter master, NVDA 2023.1. Text field started out unfocused, had to use NVDA's object navigation to get in it, tab didn't work for some reason. It still reads out the entire contents of the text field every time you change it and moving around inside will still not tell you where it's going.
The `triaged-desktop` label is irrelevant if there is no `team-desktop` label or `fyi-desktop` label.

Hello!
Any update on this?

Are there any plans to do anything with this? It's been well over 3 years, and we still can't edit text in an accessible way. For a framework which prides itself on "committed to supporting developers in making their apps more accessible", this is pretty poor.

From what I've seen of the framework code, it's not something which can be fixed in pure Dart, or I'd have already done it.

This accessibility issue has been open for nearly three years, and the lack of resolution is frankly unacceptable.
Accessibility is not an optional enhancement or a “nice-to-have” feature. It is a core requirement, especially for a framework like Flutter that actively markets itself as cross-platform, inclusive, and production-ready. Leaving a known accessibility blocker unresolved for years sends a very clear and troubling message: accessibility is not being treated as a priority.
What makes this more frustrating is that:
- The issue is well-documented
- Multiple developers have reported real-world impact
- Workarounds are either incomplete, fragile, or impossible
- No clear roadmap, ownership, or timeline has been communicated
For developers building apps for visually impaired and disabled users, this is not just technical debt — it’s a hard stop. Many of us cannot responsibly ship products while fundamental accessibility behavior is broken.
If Flutter aims to be taken seriously for large-scale and public-facing apps, then ignoring accessibility regressions for years is simply not acceptable. At the very least, this issue deserves:
- A clear acknowledgment of impact
- An official stance or explanation
- A realistic timeline or decision (even if the decision is that it won’t be fixed)
Silence and stagnation over such a long period undermine trust in the framework and force developers to reconsider whether Flutter is a viable choice for accessibility-critical applications.
Please treat this issue with the urgency and respect it deserves.

@aaryan-kumar I wholeheartedly agree. I have used Flutter to make all the custom tools we use here at Coventry Vision Hub. The nature of what we do means that most of the people who use these tools are sight issues to one degree or another. The completely blind ones are quite sick of being told that the only thing they can realistically do is write their text in Notepad then copy and paste.

On a personal note, I must confess to feeling more than a little bit resentful that so much effort goes into making everything look good, when as a screen reader user, I don't even get access to a fully-functioning text field.

> 1. Windows Hello face recognition, or fingerprint reader)

It seems we need to have biometrics enabled service for windows in order to replicate / verify it, which we don't seem to have.
I'll label this for team's attention.

@azchohfi Any thoughts about how the plugin would be affecting input focus?

@cbracken FYI
FYI, one workaround is to somehow call `SetFocus(child_content_);` in `win32_window.cpp` after the call to `authenticate()` in Dart.

Not sure why this is happening... The underlying Windows OS method (https://learn.microsoft.com/windows/win32/api/userconsentverifierinterop/nf-userconsentverifierinterop-iuserconsentverifierinterop-requestverificationforwindowasync) handles the creation of the new Window that is properly positioned as a modal HWND on top of Flutter's Root window (`::GetAncestor(view->GetNativeWindow(), GA_ROOT)`). We have access to the Root Window, so we could do what @emsaints said and call SetFocus manually, or simply send a WM_ACTIVATE to that HWND (SetActiveWindow), which will call the set focus per the default Flutter message handle loop. Not sure why this is needed, but it should fix the issue.
i fixed this by using the window_manager package, but that is just a work around ? is this already in the current version of localAuth?

Here is another example repo where the TextField breaks without biometrics: https://github.com/flutter-institute/win_textfield_test

It's just the Windows Security box popping up that seems to cause the issue. If in focus another window entirely, then refocus my app, the textbox begins working properly again.

issues.title: TextField doesn't handle trailing space as characters if textAlign is TextAlign.right/TextAlign.center
issues.body:
## Steps to Reproduce

1. Run `flutter create bug`.
2. Update main.dart as follows: ... 
```import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(MyApp());
}

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      title: 'TextField trailing space',
      theme: ThemeData(
        primarySwatch: Colors.blue,
      ),
      home: MyHomePage(title: 'Flutter Demo Home Page'),
    );
  }
}

class MyHomePage extends StatefulWidget {
  MyHomePage({Key? key, required this.title}) : super(key: key);
  final String title;

  @override
  _MyHomePageState createState() => _MyHomePageState();
}

class _MyHomePageState extends State<MyHomePage> {
  var textAlign = TextAlign.center;

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Scaffold(
      appBar: AppBar(
        title: Text(widget.title),
      ),
      body: Center(
        child: Column(
          mainAxisAlignment: MainAxisAlignment.center,
          children: <Widget>[
            Text(
              'You have pushed the button this many times:',
            ),
            TextField(
              maxLines: null,
              textAlign: textAlign,
            ),
          ],
        ),
      ),
      floatingActionButton: FloatingActionButton(
        onPressed: () {setState(() {
          textAlign = (textAlign == TextAlign.right) ? TextAlign.center : TextAlign.right;
        });},
        tooltip: 'Increment',
        child: Icon(Icons.add),
      ), 
    );
  }
}
```

3. Write a single character, then press space repeatedly until the end of line is reached. Then type a character eg 'a'.

**Expected results:** proper line break for space characters/ space characters push back input string textAlign is TextAlign.right

**Actual results:** space characters are stopped at the end of TextField and won't be handle as normal character input (break into new line, pushing the input string if textAlign is TextAlign.right).

issues.title: FocusNode does not auto scroll to TextFormField when item already selected
issues.body:
Scenario: this is a big form, the user selects the TextFormField and then manually scrolls down to the save button. if the _formKey.currentState.validate() detects validation issues we can to get the focus back the the TextFormField with issues.

first, we call _myFocusNode.unfocus(); // this works because the keyword is automatically close.
next, we call FocusScope.of(context).requestFocus(_myFocusNode) //this also works because we can start typing right after. but, **the FocusScope.of(context).requestFocus is not automatically scrolling back to the TextFormField.**

If we try to another TextFormField (Tfield2) other than the last selected, the Tfield2 gets focus and the scroll also makes it visible.

NOTE: if we start typing again, the scroll is executed and the TextFormField becomes visible. 

![Jun-06-2020 12-04-57](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5774873/83952488-066bbb00-a7ee-11ea-857e-83bbf4ff9ef7.gif)

> I found a workaround for this issue, documenting just in case another person faces the same problem,
> 
> 1. create a local ScrollController variable _workaroundScrCntrToFixFocusIssue
> 2. initialize _workaroundScrCntrToFixFocusIssue at initState()
> 3. call _workaroundScrCntrToFixFocusIssue.dispose at  dispose()
> 4. assign the _workaroundScrCntrToFixFocusIssue to your SingleChildScrollView {controller:}
> 5. when calling form validation and error are detected. Make following calls
> 
> * FocusManager.instance.primaryFocus.unfocus();
> * _workaroundScrCntrToFixFocusIssue.animateTo(0, curve: Curves.linear, duration: Duration(milliseconds: 500));
> * FocusScope.of(context).requestFocus(textfieldWithValidationIssue);
> 
> NOTE:
> in my particular use case, I just need to scroll to top of the screen, that's why I can call animateTo with position 0, if your use case is different you may need to calculate the closest location to you control.
> 
> @iapicca , please don't close this issue as this is just a workaround the auto-scrolling defect in flutter
